title: White nougat
subtitle: Honey and sugar confection with whipped egg whites and toasted almonds
excerpt: White nougat (torrone in Italian, nougat de Montélimar in French) is made by pouring a hot sugar and honey syrup cooked to hard-ball stage into stiff-whipped egg whites, producing a pale, aerated candy with a distinctive chewy snap. Almonds or pistachios are folded in before the nougat sets. The process requires a sugar thermometer and a stand mixer running throughout the syrup pour.
sourceNotes: White nougat appears across Mediterranean confectionery under several names: torrone in Italy (associated with Cremona and Sardinia), nougat de Montélimar in France (the town of Montélimar in the Drôme has had official protection for its nougat since 1942), and turrón in Spain. All are built on the same method: a hot sugar-honey syrup poured into whipped egg whites. The earliest written European recipes date from the 17th century, though the confection is older. White nougat reached Britain through confectionery imports and became a home-baking project through the early 21st century as sugar thermometers and stand mixers became common kitchen equipment.

content.text: White nougat is a pale, chewy confection of sugar syrup, honey, and egg whites, folded through with toasted almonds. It is built on an

: hot syrup poured into whipped whites, which sets the airy structure without baking. Have the egg whites whipped and the stand mixer running before the syrup reaches temperature. Once it does, it cannot wait. Pour in a slow, steady stream down the side of the bowl, not directly onto the whites. The mixture turns thick and white as it cools.
Line a 20 x 15 cm tin with baking paper. Toast the almonds and keep warm.
Put the sugar, glucose syrup, honey, and water in a saucepan over medium heat. Stir to dissolve the sugar, then cook without stirring to 130°C on a sugar thermometer.
Meanwhile, whisk the egg whites in a stand mixer to stiff peaks.

As soon as the syrup reaches 130°C, reduce the mixer to medium speed and pour the hot syrup in a thin, steady stream down the side of the bowl. Increase the speed to high and beat until the mixture is very thick, white, and holds its shape, about 5 minutes. Beat in the vanilla.
Quickly fold in the warm toasted almonds. The mixture sets fast. Scrape into the prepared tin and spread with an oiled spatula.
Cover with a sheet of baking paper and press down firmly to compact. Leave to set for at least 4 hours at room temperature.
To cut, dip a sharp knife in hot water, dry it, and cut in one motion. Store between layers of baking paper in an airtight container.
